rationale: Implied that Jon was in love with Prince Rhaegar Targaryen; devoted to his memory throughout his years of exile
Jon disliked serving Lord Robert Baratheon; blamed him for Rhaegar's death and for Jon's own exile
Named Hand of the King by King Aerys II Targaryen
Jon is haunted by his failure at the Battle of the Bells; failed to kill Robert Baratheon there, costing him the Handship
After the Battle of Ashford, Jon took over the pursuit of Robert Baratheon
Unhorsed in a joust by Ser Barristan Selmy
Jon leads a group of men in the successful taking of Griffin's Roost for the Golden Company
Killing Lord Jon Arryn's nephew and heir, Ser Denys Arryn, during the taking of Griffin's Roost
Devoted to Young Griff (Aegon Targaryen): 'I failed the father, but I will not fail the son'
